claims.claim_text: The sentence characterizes the 2019 Capital One breach as a metadata-service incident.
claims.anchor_text: The 2019 Capital One metadata-service incident (see *Node Metadata Security*) <!-- incident-xref: capital-one-2019 --> shows why static, infrastructure-bound credentials are dangerous: one compromised interface can become a pathway to broad compromise when workload identity is not dynamically validated at runtime.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The 2019 Capital One breach (see *Node Metadata Security*) <!-- incident-xref: capital-one-2019 --> shows why static, infrastructure-bound credentials are dangerous: one compromised interface can become a pathway to broad compromise when workload identity is not dynamically validated at runtime.
claims.rationale: The breach is real, but this sentence hard-codes a specific technical characterization that should either be cited elsewhere or narrowed here.

claims.claim_text: SPIFFE moved to CNCF Incubating on 2020-06-22 and Graduated on 2022-08-23.
claims.rationale: The CNCF SPIFFE project page directly lists both maturity-transition dates.

claims.claim_text: SPIRE is the most widely adopted implementation of SPIFFE.
claims.anchor_text: SPIRE (the SPIFFE Runtime Environment) is the most widely adopted implementation of the SPIFFE standard.
claims.suggested_rewrite: SPIRE (the SPIFFE Runtime Environment) is a production-ready implementation of the SPIFFE standard.
claims.rationale: The superlative adoption claim is not directly supported by an authoritative source in scope.

claims.claim_text: SPIRE is a production-ready implementation of SPIFFE and moved to CNCF Incubating on 2020-06-22 and Graduated on 2022-08-22.
claims.rationale: The CNCF SPIRE project page directly states the project status and the 2020/2022 maturity dates.

claims.claim_text: The current SPIRE release is version 1.14.5.
claims.rationale: The GitHub release page identifies v1.14.5 as the latest release.

claims.claim_text: SPIRE 1.14.5 upgraded Go to 1.26.2 to address a specific set and count of CVEs.
claims.anchor_text: SPIRE version 1.14.5 was released on 2026-04-08 and upgrades Go to 1.26.2 to address CVE-2026-32282, CVE-2026-32289, CVE-2026-33810, CVE-2026-27144, CVE-2026-27143, CVE-2026-32288, CVE-2026-32283, and CVE-2026-27140. This upgrade effectively mitigates critical supply-chain vulnerabilities within the underlying Go runtime.
claims.suggested_rewrite: SPIRE version 1.14.5 was released on 2026-04-08 and upgrades Go to 1.26.2 to address multiple Go CVEs, including CVE-2026-32282, CVE-2026-32289, CVE-2026-33810, CVE-2026-27144, CVE-2026-27143, CVE-2026-32288, CVE-2026-32283, CVE-2026-27140, and CVE-2026-32281.
claims.rationale: The release page confirms the release date, Go version, and CVE list, but the module's exact CVE count is wrong.

claims.claim_text: SPIRE 1.14.5 publishes Linux amd64-musl, Linux arm64-musl, and Windows amd64 assets with a broad-compatibility conclusion.
claims.anchor_text: Furthermore, SPIRE version 1.14.5 publishes Linux amd64-musl, Linux arm64-musl, and Windows amd64 distribution assets, ensuring broad compatibility across heterogeneous environments and enabling operators to deploy SPIRE consistently across mixed architectures.
claims.suggested_rewrite: SPIRE 1.14.5 publishes release artifacts for multiple platforms and architectures.
claims.rationale: The exact asset list and compatibility conclusion are too specific for the source coverage gathered here.

claims.claim_text: A SPIFFE ID is an RFC 3986 URI using the `spiffe` scheme, with a trust domain and path, and constrained host/path syntax.
claims.rationale: The SPIFFE ID specification directly defines the URI scheme, trust domain, path, and syntax constraints.

claims.claim_text: SPIFFE implementations must support both X.509-SVID and JWT-SVID Workload API profiles.
claims.rationale: The SPIFFE Workload API specification explicitly says both profiles are mandatory.

claims.claim_text: The SPIFFE Workload Endpoint is local-by-design, served over gRPC, and discovered via explicit configuration or `SPIFFE_ENDPOINT_SOCKET` using `unix://` or `tcp://` URIs.
claims.rationale: The Workload Endpoint specification directly covers locality, gRPC transport, and endpoint discovery semantics.

claims.claim_text: Using a local Unix socket makes network interception or remote spoofing fundamentally impossible.
claims.anchor_text: The locality of the endpoint is a critical security feature: by requiring the workload to communicate with a local agent over a Unix domain socket, SPIRE ensures that network-based interception or remote spoofing of the attestation handshake is fundamentally impossible.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The locality of the endpoint is a critical security feature: keeping the Workload Endpoint local reduces bootstrap, interception, and spoofing risk, but the exact authentication and hardening model still depends on the implementation.
claims.rationale: The standard says locality mitigates bootstrap and transport risks; it does not justify an absolute impossibility claim.

claims.claim_text: SPIRE Server includes built-in SQL datastore support for SQLite, PostgreSQL, and MySQL, plus upstream authority plugins such as Vault and AWS Private CA.
claims.rationale: The SPIRE Server configuration reference enumerates the built-in datastore and upstream authority plugins.

claims.claim_text: SQLite is sufficient only for quickstarts and small clusters, while production deployments must use PostgreSQL or MySQL for HA and concurrency at scale.
claims.anchor_text: While SQLite 3 is sufficient for quickstarts and small test clusters, enterprise deployments must utilize PostgresSQL or MySQL for high availability and to handle concurrent attestation requests at massive scale.
claims.suggested_rewrite: SQLite is a common choice for evaluation or single-node setups, while highly available production deployments typically use an external SQL backend.
claims.rationale: The docs support the available datastore backends, but this stronger deployment prescription is too specific as written.

claims.claim_text: SPIRE supports the listed node attestors and uses `k8s_psat` for Kubernetes projected service account token validation.
claims.anchor_text: SPIRE supports node attestors for AWS (EC2 Identity Document), Azure managed service identities, GCE instance tokens, Kubernetes Service Account tokens, and join-token fallback where tokens expire immediately after use. On Kubernetes environments, the Server must validate the Agent's projected ServiceAccount token using the `k8s_psat` plugin.
claims.suggested_rewrite: SPIRE includes built-in node attestors such as `aws_iid`, `azure_imds`/`azure_msi`, `gcp_iit`, `k8s_psat`, and `join_token`; on Kubernetes, the server validates projected ServiceAccount tokens with the `k8s_psat` plugin.
claims.rationale: The built-in attestor list is supported, but the sentence adds unsupported specificity about join-token expiry behavior.

claims.claim_text: JWT-SVIDs are bearer tokens, must carry `aud`, and are replayable if intercepted; strict audience scoping limits replay scope.
claims.rationale: The JWT-SVID specification directly defines the `aud` requirement and its replay and transport-security implications.

claims.claim_text: A SPIRE registration entry is defined by a SPIFFE ID, selectors, and a parent ID.
claims.rationale: The `spire-server entry create` reference directly lists `-spiffeID`, `-selector`, and `-parentID`.

claims.claim_text: The module states as an absolute rule that a registration entry may use either node selectors or workload selectors, but not both.
claims.span_hint: line 170; line 243; quiz answer at lines 537-538 is out of scope
claims.anchor_text: | **Mixing selector types** | A registration entry may use either node selectors or workload selectors, but not both. Combining them violates the specification. | Create distinct registration entries linked via a parent ID. |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Mixing selector types** | Keep node and workload identity concerns in separate entries linked via a parent ID, instead of collapsing them into one overly broad rule. | Create distinct registration entries linked via a parent ID. |
claims.rationale: The architectural guidance is sound, but the strict specification wording is not directly supported by the sources gathered.

claims.claim_text: SPIRE Controller Manager / Workload Registrar automates registration entry creation from Pods or `ClusterSPIFFEID` resources.
claims.rationale: The controller-manager README directly describes reconciliation from Pods and `ClusterSPIFFEID` resources into SPIRE entries.

claims.claim_text: SPIRE federation uses a bundle endpoint plus `federates_with`, and the `https_spiffe` profile requires an expected remote SPIFFE ID.
claims.rationale: The server configuration reference directly documents `bundle_endpoint`, `federates_with`, and `https_spiffe`/`endpoint_spiffe_id`.

claims.claim_text: SPIRE provides an Envoy Secret Discovery Service implementation for fetching and rotating certificates and bundles.
claims.rationale: The SPIRE repository README explicitly states that SPIRE implements Envoy SDS.

claims.claim_text: `spiffe-helper` fetches X.509 SVIDs from the Workload API, writes certificate material to disk, and signals a process for reload.
claims.rationale: The SPIFFE Helper README directly describes fetch, file output, and renewal signaling behavior.

claims.claim_text: The hostPID table row asserts a specific workload-spoofing mechanism and a Pod Security Standards-based fix.
claims.anchor_text: | **Running pods with `hostPID: true`** | The pod shares the host's PID namespace, allowing it to inspect other processes' metadata. A malicious workload can connect to the Workload API and spoof another process's PID to claim its SVID. | Block `hostPID` via Pod Security Standards cluster-wide. |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Running pods with `hostPID: true`** | Sharing the host PID namespace weakens the assumptions behind PID-based workload attestation and should be avoided for SPIRE-managed workloads. | Restrict `hostPID` where your cluster security policy allows. |
claims.rationale: The hardening lesson is valid, but the row's exact attack narrative and remediation framing are more specific than the sources directly support.

claims.claim_text: The upstream CA rotation row asserts silent CA-rotation failure, catastrophic mesh failure, and a specific metric name.
claims.anchor_text: | **Ignoring Upstream CA rotation** | If the upstream CA connection fails, SPIRE Server silently stops rotating its own CA certificate, leading to a catastrophic mesh failure. | Monitor and alert on the `spire_server_ca_cert_expiry_seconds` metric. |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Ignoring Upstream CA rotation** | If upstream CA integration stays broken long enough, server CA rotation can fail and eventually disrupt identity issuance. | Monitor server CA expiration using the telemetry exposed by your SPIRE deployment. |
claims.rationale: The operational risk is real, but the exact failure characterization and metric name are not directly backed by the sources gathered.

claims.claim_text: Unencrypted JWT-SVID transmission allows interception and replay-based impersonation.
claims.rationale: The JWT-SVID security considerations explicitly describe bearer-token interception and replay risk.

claims.claim_text: The hardcoded socket-path row asserts CSI path instability across updates and recommends `SPIFFE_ENDPOINT_SOCKET`.
claims.anchor_text: | **Hardcoding API socket paths** | CSI drivers may mount sockets to unexpected paths across updates, breaking workloads that hardcode `/tmp/agent.sock`. | Use the `SPIFFE_ENDPOINT_SOCKET` environment variable dynamically within application containers. |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Hardcoding API socket paths** | Avoid hardcoding Workload API socket paths; consume the socket location from runtime configuration such as `SPIFFE_ENDPOINT_SOCKET`. | Use the `SPIFFE_ENDPOINT_SOCKET` environment variable dynamically within application containers. |
claims.rationale: The environment-variable guidance is supported, but the row's exact CSI update scenario is not directly substantiated.

claims.claim_text: The hostPath row asserts a specific inode-detachment failure mode and prescribes the SPIFFE CSI driver.
claims.anchor_text: | **Using `hostPath` for the Workload API** | Restarting the DaemonSet changes the socket inode on the host, silently detaching running workloads from the new API endpoint. | Utilize the official `spiffe-csi-driver` for proper socket lifecycle management and mount propagation. |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Using `hostPath` for the Workload API** | Avoid exposing the Workload API through ad hoc `hostPath` mounts when a supported CSI integration is available. | Utilize the official `spiffe-csi-driver` for proper socket lifecycle management and mount propagation. |
claims.rationale: The lifecycle-management recommendation is reasonable, but the exact inode-failure story is too specific for the source support gathered.

claims.claim_text: The practitioner-gotcha context uses exact scale numbers and an architectural claim about eventual consistency.
claims.anchor_text: **Context**: At massive scale (thousands of nodes, tens of thousands of pods), the time it takes for a new registration entry to propagate from the SPIRE Server to the local agent can cause early pod startup failures. The SPIRE architecture prioritizes eventual consistency over immediate synchronous blocking.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Context**: In large deployments, propagation delay for a new registration entry can cause early pod startup failures before the local agent sees the update.
claims.rationale: The operational lesson is plausible, but the exact scale figures and architectural characterization are too specific as written.

claims.claim_text: A workload that gets `no identity issued` / no identity yet may need retry/backoff because SPIFFE implementations can be eventually consistent.
claims.rationale: The Workload Endpoint spec says `PermissionDenied` may be retried with backoff because implementations can be eventually consistent.

claims.claim_text: The exercise claims the SPIRE Kubernetes quickstart is tested on 1.29-1.34 and fully functional on Kubernetes 1.35.
claims.anchor_text: Ensure the cluster is running a compatible Kubernetes version. Note that the SPIRE Kubernetes quickstart has been tested with Kubernetes versions 1.29 through 1.34, but it is fully functional on Kubernetes version 1.35.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Ensure the cluster is running a Kubernetes version that is supported by the SPIRE release and installation method you are using.
claims.rationale: The version-range and forward-compatibility claim is too specific to keep without a directly matching upstream compatibility statement.

further_reading.title: SPIFFE Workload Endpoint
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the core spec for locality, gRPC transport, endpoint discovery, and retry semantics.

further_reading.title: JWT SPIFFE Verifiable Identity Document
further_reading.why_relevant: It explains `aud`, replay risk, bearer-token transport, and validation rules for JWT-SVIDs.

further_reading.title: SPIRE Server Configuration Reference
further_reading.why_relevant: It documents federation, datastore backends, upstream authorities, and server-side operational knobs.

further_reading.title: NIST SP 800-207 Zero Trust Architecture
further_reading.why_relevant: It provides the identity-centric zero-trust framing that motivates workload identity on-premises.

notes: Local cold-start API was unavailable (`api: FAILED to start — see logs/api.log`), so the shared section pool was read directly from `docs/citation-pools/on-premises-security.json` and claims were validated against live CNCF/GitHub primary sources.
